elements.sent-2411-001: There is growing interest in applying AI to radiology report generation, particularly for chest X-rays (CXRs).
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper investigates whether incorporating pixel-level information through segmentation masks can improve fine-grained image interpretation of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for radiology report generation.
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce MAIRA-Seg, a segmentation-aware MLLM framework designed to utilize semantic segmentation masks alongside CXRs for generating radiology reports.
elements.sent-2411-004: We train expert segmentation models to obtain mask pseudolabels for radiology-specific structures in CXRs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Subsequently, building on the architectures of MAIRA, a CXR-specialised model for report generation, we integrate a trainable segmentation tokens extractor that leverages these mask pseudolabels, and employ mask-aware prompting to generate draft radiology reports.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments on the \review{publicly available MIMIC-CXR dataset show that MAIRA-Seg outperforms non-segmentation baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We also investigate set-of-marks prompting with MAIRA and find that MAIRA-Seg consistently demonstrates comparable or superior performance.
elements.sent-2411-008: The results confirm that using segmentation masks enhances the nuanced reasoning of MLLMs, potentially contributing to better clinical outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-009: We use the MIMIC-CXR dataset \citep{johnson2019mimic-cxr,johnson2019mimic-cxr-dataset and CXR segmentation datasets which are all publicly available, referring to~\sectionref{expert_models and ~\sectionref{sec:data for further details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: At present, we do not release the code.
elements.sent-2411-011: Proposed use of public datasets was reviewed by home institution.
elements.sent-2411-012: Under policy, use of de-identified public datasets is classified as Not Human Subjects Research [per 45§46.102(e)(1)(ii), 45§46.102(e)(5)].
elements.sent-2411-013: Guidance and data reflection questions are provided to researchers including considerations to support representativeness, transparency and intended use.
elements.sent-2411-014: Radiology report generation involves the automated creation of free-text draft reports from medical images~\citep{liu2019clinicallyaccurate .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Research indicates that applying \ac{AI in this area could significantly enhance radiology workflows and clinical outcomes~\citep{huang2023generative, yildrim2024multimodal .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: There is increasing interest in using \acfp{MLLM for \acf{CXR report generation, demonstrating impressive performance~\citep{tu2024medpalmm, chaves2024towards, zhou2024generalist, bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport, hyland2024maira1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, current \acp{MLLM often neglect the integration of pixel-level inputs alongside images for generating radiology reports, limiting their region-based and fine-grained image interpretation capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This is particularly significant in the biomedical domain, where a single medical image can contain multiple \review{subtle findings , nuanced structures and relevant context representing the \acp{ROI .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: This gap presents an opportunity to enhance \ac{MLLM outputs by incorporating segmentation masks, which we aim to explore in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-020: We hypothesize that providing localized pixel-level details alongside images can enhance \ac{MLLM 's perceptual and reasoning abilities for biomedical applications like radiology report generation.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We propose MAIRA-Seg, a segmentation-aware \ac{MLLM that utilizes fine-grained mask features from semantic medical image segmentation alongside CXR input images to generate draft radiology reports.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We build upon the model architectures and training method from the \ac{MAIRA series of \acp{MLLM ~\citep{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport, hyland2024maira1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: To the best of our knowledge, ours is the first work leveraging semantic image segmentation for instruction tuning \acp{MLLM for \ac{CXR report generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: By integrating pixel-level knowledge in the form of segmentation and mask-aware information into the prompt instructions of the \ac{MLLM , we aim to improve the pixel-wise visual understanding and enhance the quality and accuracy of draft radiology reports generated from \acp{CXR .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our contributions are: 1) We propose MAIRA-Seg, a segmentation aware framework for radiology report generation.
elements.sent-2411-026: Semantic segmentation masks generated from expert models are integrated into the \ac{MLLM input using a segmentation tokens extractor, enabling fine-grained supervision along with \ac{CXR images.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: By incorporating semantic segmentation as additional visual inputs along with single or multiple \ac{CXR views, we achieve superior performance in radiology report generation.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We demonstrate notable quantitative and qualitative improvements of MAIRA-Seg over the non-segmentation baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-029: Here, visual marks (e.g., contours) are directly superimposed on the image for visual instruction tuning.
elements.sent-2411-030: For \ac{SoM , we observe improvements over the non-segmentation baselines and comparable performance to MAIRA-Seg.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Recent advances in \ac{AI for generating free-text radiology reports suggest improvements in operational efficiency, reduction in radiologist workloads, and enhancement of patient care quality ~\citep{huang2023generative, yildrim2024multimodal, liu2019clinicallyaccurate .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: As a result, there has been a growing research interest in the generation of free-text, narrative-style reports from radiology images~\citep{sloan2024automated .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Specifically, \acp{MLLM have been increasingly explored and have demonstrated a promising performance for radiology report generation.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Recent \acp{MLLM in the literature encompass generalist biomedical models for multiple imaging modalities~\citep{zhou2024generalist, tu2024medpalmm, yang2024advancing and specialist radiology domain models \citep{hyland2024maira1, bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport, li2024holisticframeworkmultimodallarge, bai2024m3dadvancing3dmedical, chaves2024towards .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: For this work, we build on the \ac{CXR -specific MAIRA framework~\citep{hyland2024maira1, bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport , \review{as it has demonstrated competitive \ac{CXR report generation performance over prior works such as LLaVA-Rad~\citep{chaves2024towards , MedPalm-M~\citep{tu2024medpalmm and MedVersa~\citep{zhou2024generalist .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Recent work in the general domain shows that using \review{visual prompts (e.g. bounding boxes, markers, segmentation masks) with images for \ac{MLLM visual instruction tuning can enhance their visual perception capabilities ~\citep{wu2024visualpromptingmultimodallarge .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, among methods using segmentation masks, the \acf{SoM prompting method~\citep{yang2023setofmark uses off-the-shelf interactive segmentation models to partition the input image into semantically meaningful regions using ``marks'' (e.g., bounding boxes, contours, alphanumeric marks).}}
elements.sent-2411-038: They query GPT-4V using such marked images and observe that it can provide visually grounded zero-shot responses.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, ~\citet{yan2024listitemsonenew presents a method to enhance \ac{SoM prompting ability of existing \acp{MLLM by using augmented prompts that incorporate mark information.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Datasets of \ac{SoM -augmented images and prompts are curated for fine-tuning MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We conduct ablations and comparative analysis on \ac{SoM prompting with MAIRA-Seg and report our key findings.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Another approach, the Osprey method~\citep{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual , uses a mask-aware visual extractor to obtain segmentation tokens interleaved with image tokens to prompt the \ac{MLLM , which is fine-tuned using a curated visual instruction tuning dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We adapt the Osprey architecture for the radiology domain (details in \sectionref{sec:methods ).}
elements.sent-2411-044: In contrast to Osprey and \ac{SoM , we generate online mask-aware prompts without the need to generate new instruction tuning datasets for generating the radiology reports.}
elements.sent-2411-045: For radiology images, recent work~\citep{denner2024visualpromptengineeringmedical shows that incorporating visual prompts like arrows, circles, and contours for BiomedCLIP models significantly improves lung nodule malignancy classification metrics in \acp{CXR .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Another related study~\citep{zhao2023medicalreportgenerationbased uses the \ac{SAM ~\citep{kirillov2023segment to segment meaningful \acp{ROI of the image and a supervised contrastive learning method showing promising performance for the report generation task.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In our knowledge, ours is the first paper leveraging segmentation masks in \acp{MLLM to improve their nuanced visual understanding for radiology report generation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The MAIRA-Seg architecture and method are demonstrated in \figureref{fig:method .}
elements.sent-2411-049: We first train structure-specific expert models for segmenting multiple \ac{CXR structures.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We use these models to generate segmentation masks for the \ac{CXR images and feed these as pseudolabel inputs to the \ac{MLLM for training or inference.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The masks along with image encoder features are then used to train a \extractor based on the Osprey architecture~\citep{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual that generates two additional segmentation tokens (mask token, spatial token) for each individual mask.}
elements.sent-2411-052: We investigate methods to integrate these tokens into the \ac{LLM 's input, and use interleaved segmentation tokens with text and image tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-053: We augment the input prompts on the fly using the available mask information, without the overhead of curating new instruction tuning datasets to train the \ac{MLLM .}
elements.sent-2411-054: We describe the individual components of the proposed method in the following sections.
elements.figure-2411-001: \floatconts {fig:method} {\caption{(a) MAIRA-Seg model architecture. Multi-view and textual context inputs are shown in blue boxes~\citep{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport}. (b) Segmentation tokens extractor architecture based on \citet{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual}.}} {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth, clip, trim=0cm 0cm 10cm 0cm]{images/method_n7.pdf}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Expert Models for Semantic Medical Image Segmentation
elements.sent-2411-055: We leverage expert semantic segmentation models trained for segmenting multiple structures in \acp{CXR , and predict the corresponding semantic segmentation masks in the input radiology image.}
elements.sent-2411-056: For semantic segmentation, we select structures that are relevant to common pathological observations in \acp{CXR (e.g. CheXpert pathological findings~\citep{chexpert ), and can improve the pixel-level understanding and reasoning capabilities of the trained \ac{MLLM for \ac{CXR report generation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We group these structures into three main categories: anatomical, pathological and support devices (\sectionref{app:seg , \tableref{tab:seg_data ).}}
elements.sent-2411-058: We note that the trained expert semantic segmentation models perform adequately for the respective structures (\sectionref{app:seg , \tableref{tab:seg_results ).}}
elements.sent-2411-059: There has been an influx of general-purpose, prompt-based (bounding box, points, text) segmentation models in the biomedical domain, for instance, MedSAM~\citep{Ma_2024 and BiomedPARSE~\citep{zhao2024biomedparsebiomedicalfoundationmodel .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Compared to a U-Net, these prompt-based models require additional input prompts to segment the structures of interest in the \ac{CXR .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Since we strive for a fully automated system for \ac{CXR report generation without relying on input prompts, we used the `segment everything' approach of these models, and during our initial experiments, we found that these models were sub-optimal compared to domain- and structure-specific U-Nets.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Hence, general-purpose, prompt-based segmentation models may not yield precise segmentation for clinically critical tasks like draft radiology report generation.
elements.sent-2411-063: We leverage the MAIRA framework ~\citep{hyland2024maira1, bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport to experiment with semantic segmentation as additional visual inputs along with \ac{CXR images for generating draft reports.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: MAIRA \acp{MLLM use a pretrained \ac{CXR -specific image encoder (\raddino) based on a ViT-B architecture~\citep{pérezgarcía2024raddinoexploringscalablemedical and a pretrained \ac{LLM Vicuna-7B v1.5~\citep{vicuna2023 , and align these using a 4-layer \ac{MLP adapter based on the LLaVA framework~\citep{liu2023llava, liu2023llava15 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The \ac{CXR -specific \raddino~\citep{pérezgarcía2024raddinoexploringscalablemedical image encoder is pretrained following the self-supervised DINOv2 approach~\citep{oquab2023dinov2 ; it is kept frozen during the \ac{MLLM training.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We use the image encoder checkpoint from~\citet{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The \ac{LLM and adapter are fine-tuned in a single stage to generate the draft radiology report.}
elements.sent-2411-068: MAIRA-Seg follows the MAIRA architecture for leveraging image and text inputs (\figureref{fig:method (a)), with some simplifications aimed at reducing the computational needs.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We replace the \ac{LLM with Phi-3-mini consisting of 3.8B parameters~\citep{abdin2024phi3technicalreporthighly , which demonstrated performance close to Vicuna-7B in recent work~\citep{srivastav-etal-2024-maira .}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In addition, we do not apply GPT augmentations at training time.
elements.sent-2411-071: Also, we don't perform grounding in our experiments as in~\citet{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-072: As detailed in \sectionref{sec:data , the amount of data we train on differs.}
elements.sent-2411-073: We experiment with two flavours of MAIRA-Seg.
elements.sent-2411-074: A single-view model \mairasegone that takes as input only the frontal chest x-ray and their corresponding mask and reports; and a multi-view model \mairasegtwo that takes as input also priors and laterals, if available, and the corresponding masks.
elements.sent-2411-075: In the \mairasegtwo model we also incorporate additional `textual context' (prior report, indication, technique and comparison sections of the current report) in the text prompts as in~\citep{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In order to leverage the mask inputs, we incorporate into the \ac{MAIRA framework, a trainable \extractor module based on the Osprey method that uses a visually-aware mask extractor~\citep{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The \extractor utilizes the output image features from \raddino (ViT-B architecture)~\cite{pérezgarcía2024raddinoexploringscalablemedical and individual segmentation masks to generate visually-aware mask and spatial tokens~\citep{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual .}}
elements.sent-2411-078: A mask token (for one structure) is obtained as a result of mask pooling operation between image and mask features followed by linear projections, addition and \ac{MLP , to capture mask-level image features.}
elements.sent-2411-079: A spatial token is obtained via flattening and linear projection of the segmentation mask only which extracts the spatial location of the structure of interest.
elements.sent-2411-080: We collectively refer to the mask and spatial tokens as `segmentation tokens'.
elements.sent-2411-081: Since this approach involves the extraction of only two additional segmentation tokens per structure, it is scalable to extend to multiple structures and hence masks in the image -- a common scenario found in biomedical images with multiple regions of interest, such as \acp{CXR .}
elements.sent-2411-082: This flexibility makes the approach suitable for fixed computational budgets and context lengths of \acp{LLM .}
elements.sent-2411-083: A major difference from the Osprey method~\citep{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual is that we use a transformer-based image encoder \raddino~(ViT-B backbone) in contrast to a \ac{CNN -based CLIP vision encoder used in~\citet{yuan2024ospreypixelunderstandingvisual .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We empirically investigate four design considerations to integrate the the segmentation tokens extractor with MAIRA \acp{MLLM : 1) the interchangeability of linear and \ac{MLP layers within the original mask extractor, 2) embedding dimension sizes, 3) layer output indices from the ViT-B based image encoder, and 4) the number of segmentation tokens used to prompt the \ac{LLM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We select linear layers for both segmentation tokens, smaller embedding dimension of 768 (opposed to 1024 in the Osprey method), and layer indices [2,4,6,8] for ViT-B (in contrast to first four layers of the convolutional encoder in Osprey method).
elements.sent-2411-086: We use two segmentation tokens, namely one token for the mask information and one for spatial information (similar to Osprey method), opposed to fewer (1 token) or more (4 or 8 tokens).
elements.sent-2411-087: We propose the architecture of the segmentation tokens extractor as shown in \figureref{fig:method (b).}
elements.sent-2411-088: The segmentation tokens extractor, the MLP adapter and \ac{LLM are all finetuned, while the \raddino image encoder is frozen during the \ac{MLLM training.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: More implementation details for MAIRA-Seg models are in~\sectionref{apd:third .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The segmentation tokens retrieved from the \extractor are used to prompt the \ac{LLM along with the image and text tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-091: We explore multiple approaches to incorporate segmentation tokens as input of the \ac{LLM , including directly concatenating all image and segmentation tokens, concatenating segmentation tokens in addition to the image tokens, and using separate segmentation tokens for individual structures in the image.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Best outcomes are achieved using separate segmentation tokens for individual structures in the image where the structure-specific tokens interleave with the image and text tokens in the input prompt.
elements.sent-2411-094: Every segmentation token is not always used in the text prompt, as the input tokens are only added when a positive segmentation mask is available.
elements.sent-2411-095: Here, positive masks are defined as binary masks with at least one `1' pixel.
elements.sent-2411-096: We perform online mask-aware prompting using input mask information, i.e. the structure names when a positive mask is available, followed by the corresponding segmentation tokens.
elements.sent-2411-097: This strategy helps us to quickly prototype without the need to curate new instruction tuning datasets to train the \ac{MLLM .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Lateral image, prior image and textual context are added in the \mairasegtwo prompt, similar to ~\citet{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-099: An example of a text prompt of the \mairasegtwo model for a study with current and prior frontal images and corresponding positive masks is: `You are an expert radiology assistant tasked with interpreting a chest X-ray study.
elements.sent-2411-100: Given the current frontal image <Image>, left lung mask <LLseg>, right lung mask <RLseg>, endotracheal tube mask <ETTseg>, heart mask <Heartseg> and the prior frontal image <PriorImage>, prior left lung mask <priorLLseg>, prior right lung mask <PriorRLseg>, prior heart mask <PriorHeartseg>, provide a description of the findings in the radiology study in comparison to the prior frontal image.
elements.sent-2411-101: Where segmentation masks are provided to highlight specific image regions...<textual context>'.
elements.sent-2411-102: We address the task of generating the main body section \findings{ of the text report accompanying a chest X-ray.}
elements.sent-2411-103: The task is identical to the radiology report generation task reported in~\citet{hyland2024maira1 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: We perform experiments for single-and multi-view MAIRA-Seg and \ac{SoM , and compare with their respective baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-105: We perform the \ac{MLLM report generation experiments using the MIMIC-CXR dataset \citep{johnson2019mimic-cxr,johnson2019mimic-cxr-dataset hosted on PhysioNet \citep{goldberger2000physiobank .}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: This dataset from the Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Boston comprises a total of 377,110 DICOM images across 227,835 studies.
elements.sent-2411-107: Each imaging study is accompanied by a report.
elements.sent-2411-108: For each report, we extract the \findings{ section using the official MIMIC-CXR codebase \footnote{\url{https://github.com/MIT-LCP/mimic-cxr/blob/master/txt/section_parser.py .}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: We train all models on the training split of MIMIC-CXR and report results on the official MIMIC-CXR test split using standard lexical and clinical metrics~\citep{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-110: We also report additional Mask-Relevant (MR) clinical metrics (macro and micro F1-MR) on the CheXpert pathological findings relevant to the segmented structures, namely `Lung Opacity', `Cardiomegaly', `Pneumothorax', `Support Devices', `Pleural Effusion'.
elements.sent-2411-111: These pathological findings are directly correlated with the input segmentation masks (\sectionref{app:seg , \tableref{tab:seg_data ).}}
elements.sent-2411-112: It is worth noting that our macro and micro F1-MR are different from the standard macro and micro F1-5~\citep{miura2021improvingfactualcompletenessconsistency due to the difference in the selected pathological findings for analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-113: We select BLEU-4, RadCliQ, \macromr, \micromr and Radfact/logical\_F1 for further analysis in the paper, with additional metrics in Appendix tables.
elements.sent-2411-114: We present the median along with 95\% confidence intervals calculated from 500 bootstrap samples.
elements.sent-2411-115: Bold indicates best performance for that metric, or overlapping CIs with best, compared to the baselines.
elements.sent-2411-116: All metrics are higher is better except where `$\downarrow$' indicates lower is better.
elements.sent-2411-117: CheXpert F1 metrics are computed based on CheXbert labeller outputs~\citep{hyland2024maira1 .}
elements.sent-2411-118: We compare MAIRA-Seg against baseline models, trained solely on input \ac{CXR images without using semantic segmentation masks, referred to as \mairaonebaseline and \mairatwobaseline.}

F1-MR* are F1 scores on the mask-relevant CheXpert pathological findings, namely, `Lung Opacity', `Cardiomegaly', `Pneumothorax', `Support Devices', `Pleural Effusion'.} \centerline{ \begin{tabular}{@{}p{3.3cm}p{2cm}p{2.2cm}p{2.4cm}p{2.4cm}p{3.1cm}@{}} Method BLEU-4 RadCliQ(↓) \macromr* \micromr* RadFact/logical\_f1 \mairaonebaseline 14.2 [13.7, 14.8] 3.19 [3.15, 3.22] 54.1 [51.2, 56.2] 61.4 [60.0, 62.7] 42.4 [41.5, 43.6] \somone 14.6 [14.1, 15.2] 3.14 [3.10, 3.18] 55.3 [53.1, 57.8] 63.1 [61.9, 64.4] 44.0 [43.0, 45.1] \mairasegone 14.5 [14.0, 15.1] 3.11 [3.08, 3.15] 59.2 [56.7, 61.7] 65.4 [64.1, 66.7] 44.7 [43.8, 45.8] \mairatwobaseline 19.5 [19.0, 20.1] 2.90 [2.86, 2.94] 52.5 [50.0, 55.0] 55.6 [54.2, 57.0] 45.4 [44.2, 46.5] \somtwo 20.3 [19.8, 20.8] 2.81 [2.77, 2.85] 53.9 [51.5, 56.3] 59.9 [58.6, 61.1] 47.4 [46.3, 48.6] \mairasegtwo 19.6 [19.1, 20.1] 2.82 [2.78, 2.86] 55.9 [53.6, 58.7] 60.9 [59.6, 62.5] 47.1 [46.1, 48.3] \end{tabular} } \label{tab:maira_test}

Mask pseudolabels are shown overlaid on the \ac{CXR} image for illustrative purposes (corresponding masks are used to obtain segmentation tokens).}} {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth, clip, trim=20pt 9cm 10pt 20pt]{images/maira1_main.pdf}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Ablation of strategies for incorporating segmentation tokens on MIMIC-CXR validation split. elements.sent-2411-135: We explore multiple approaches to incorporate segmentation tokens as input of the \ac{LLM .}
elements.sent-2411-136: These include 1) concatenating image and segmentation features in unified image tokens, where the image and text tokens are interleaved (DC: direct concatenation); 2) concatenating segmentation tokens from all the structures in the image, where the combined segmentation tokens are interleaved with the image and text tokens (CS: Combined segmentation tokens); 3) using separate segmentation tokens for individual structures in the image, where the structure-specific tokens interleave with the image and text tokens (SS: structure-specific tokens).
elements.sent-2411-137: Results are depicted in \tableref{tab:osprey_ablations for the MIMIC-CXR validation split.}
elements.sent-2411-138: We observe that the third strategy (SS) gives the best results and we select this strategy for introducing segmentation tokens at the input of the \ac{LLM .}
elements.sent-2411-139: We also perform ablations on the impact of segmentation tokens and mask-aware prompting (\tableref{tab:osprey_ablations , third and fourth columns), and observe improvements from the baseline for both cases, highlighting the importance of both sources of information to train the \ac{MLLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-140: In \tableref{tab:maira_test , we observe significant improvements over the \mairatwobaseline for 3 out of 4 clinical metrics using both \somtwo or \mairasegtwo to leverage segmentation in \acp{MLLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Macro F1-MR improvements are not significant in both cases, which could be attributed to data imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-142: We find \somtwo is closer in performance to \mairasegtwo, in contrast to the single-view experiments.
elements.sent-2411-143: This can be due to the fact that our architecture choices are based on ablation and tuning experiments using the single view counterparts (fewer input tokens to the \ac{LLM ).}
elements.sent-2411-144: Also, using a larger number of input tokens in \mairasegtwo with the same-sized LLM could lead to more complex interactions, unlike \somtwo, which maintains a constant number of image tokens.
elements.sent-2411-145: Thus, we report \mairasegtwo results as proof-of-concept with significant gains over \mairatwobaseline and consistent findings with \mairasegone, however, there is potential for enhancing the \mairasegtwo architecture for optimal performance which we leave for future work.
elements.sent-2411-146: For the pathology-stratified F1-MR scores in~\figureref{fig:stratified (b), we observe significant improvements for \mairasegtwo from the non-segmentation baseline for most relevant pathological findings such as support devices, cardiomegaly and pleural effusion.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Observing the qualitative results, in \sectionref{apd:second , \figureref{fig:qual4 (a), \mairasegtwo and \mairatwobaseline correctly mention stable cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions and left retrocardiac opacity.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: However, \mairatwobaseline hallucinates a right-sided PICC line, which is not mentioned by \mairasegtwo, also not detected as a mask by the expert segmentation model in current or prior images.
elements.sent-2411-150: Improvement of aeration is omitted in both predicted draft reports.
elements.sent-2411-151: Although \ac{SoM prompting shows competitive results and also outperform the non-segmentation baselines in several instances, we noted several challenges with such a prompting method for biomedical images.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Firstly, as multiple masks are overlaid on the image in the form of alphanumeric characters and/or contours, these may lead to undesirable occlusions in the image -- while this may not be problematic in natural scenes, it could be consequential for biomedical images containing small structures (e.g. rib fractures or lung lesions in \ac{CXR images).}
elements.sent-2411-153: Moreover, as we scale-up to multiple structures in a single image, it may increase the number of occlusions and lead to confusions in the attended image features by the~\ac{MLLM (e.g. multiple overlapping lines, overlapping anatomical regions with tubes).}
elements.sent-2411-154: In contrast, it is easier to scale up MAIRA-Seg to more structures, which is only limited by the context length of the \ac{LLM .}
elements.sent-2411-155: Lastly, it is unclear if the frozen \raddino image encoder~\citep{pérezgarcía2024raddinoexploringscalablemedical trained on \acp{CXR can interpret out-of-domain images with the set of marks.}}
elements.sent-2411-156: We presented MAIRA-Seg, a proof-of-concept \ac{MLLM framework to leverage semantic segmentation masks for \ac{CXR radiology report generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Our experiments demonstrate improvements in clinical metrics (with additional mask-relevant metrics) using the proposed approach on MAIRA architectures.
elements.sent-2411-158: We observe encouraging improvements in quantitative results for MAIRA-Seg when introducing segmentation masks with \ac{CXR images to visually prompt the \ac{MLLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-159: This confirms that segmentation can enhance the nuanced fine-granular reasoning and understanding of MAIRA-Seg to interpret complex biomedical images.
elements.sent-2411-160: We compare our method for single- and multi-view baseline models, where we outperform the latter in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-161: However, set-of-marks also achieves competitive metrics over the non-segmentation baselines, reconfirming our hypothesis that semantic segmentation can potentially lead to better \ac{MLLM understanding of radiological images and generation of superior draft reports.}
elements.sent-2411-162: In future, we intend to explore ways to improve the segmentation quality of the generated mask pseudo-labels (e.g. there may be under or over-segmentation), especially for the fine-grained structures.
elements.sent-2411-163: Moreover, we will explore more generalist segmentation models in our framework in order to scale up to multiple unseen structures without sacrificing segmentation performance.
elements.sent-2411-164: We intend to perform more elaborate assessment of results through human-centric evaluation, similar to the study described in~\citet{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-165: Lastly, we aim to continue exploring the interactions between the visual (images, segmentation) and text tokens in \acp{MLLM .. .}
elements.sent-2411-166: To utilize the available computational requirements economically for fixed computational budgets, we perform ablations and tuning experiments using the \mairasegone architecture which uses only frontal images at the input.
elements.sent-2411-167: We then transfer key findings to \mairasegtwo with multi-view inputs including current lateral image, prior image, additional report sections (see \figureref{fig:method (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-168: We report our ablation results on the MIMIC-CXR validation split~\citep{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Results of ablations on set-of-marks prompting are depicted in \tableref{tab:somablations for the MIMIC-CXR validation split.}
elements.sent-2411-170: We observe that mask-aware augmented prompts help compared to settings without prompt augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-171: Also, presence of contours and alphanumeric marks are more beneficial than just contours, consistent with the observations in~\citep{yang2023setofmark .}
elements.sent-2411-172: We also performed preliminary sanity checks where we used the same grayscale intensity for contours, or the same alphanumeric mark, for different masks, however these settings lead to worse performance compared to the classic \ac{SoM method.}

elements.sent-2411-196: We train MAIRA-Seg models (\mairasegone and \mairasegtwo) with a conventional autoregressive cross-entropy loss on the MIMIC-CXR training set.
elements.sent-2411-197: Following the training method in~\citet{hyland2024maira1 , we do a single stage of training with a frozen image encoder and trainable adapter and \ac{LLM .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: We train for 3 epochs and use the final checkpoint in evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-199: We use the AdamW optimiser and a cosine learning rate scheduler.
elements.sent-2411-200: We train \mairasegone on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a global training batch size of 128, training time $\approx$15h, warm-up of 0.03, and a learning rate of \num{2e-5 .}
elements.sent-2411-201: We train \mairasegtwo across 16 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a global training batch size of 256, training time $\approx$11h, warmup ratio 0.03, and a learning rate of \num{2e-5 .}
elements.sent-2411-202: We use a pre-trained \raddino image encoder~\citep{pérezgarcía2024raddinoexploringscalablemedical using the same weights as used in~\citet{bannur2024maira2groundedradiologyreport .}}
elements.sent-2411-203: We use a training batch size 80 (10 per GPU), Adam optimizer, base learning rate \num{5e-4 , and a cosine learning rate scheduler.}
elements.sent-2411-204: We use the following preprocessing and augmentations: centre-cropping and resizing (512 $\times$ 512), random horizontal flip (except left–right lungs), random affine transform, elastic transform, random brightness and contrast jittering, and random gamma adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-205: The segmentation models are trained for 100 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-206: The checkpoint with minimum loss on the validation set is used for inference on the test set.
elements.sent-2411-207: We use a 70/15/15 split by subjects for train, validation and test sets, respectively, and report metrics on the test set.
